Location_Tags: Rico, Dolores County, Durango, La Plata County
Description: I Lived in the Rico Depot is an oral history presented by life-long Colorado resident, Joanne Branson Smith. Joanne's earliest memories of childhood are of her adventures in and around the Denver Rio Grande Southern (RGS) railroad depot in Rico, CO. Rico is a small hamlet high in the mountains of central Colorado. It was once was a bustling boom town as the surrounding mountains swarmed with miners hunting gold, silver, copper, zinc and other metals in the late 19th century. Joanne weaves a story that tells of the symbiosis of the mining and railroad industries that made some at the turn of the 20th century deliriously wealthy. Joanne's mother was the manager of the depot while her father labored underground as a coal miner. After the railroad folded, Edgar Branson worked as a carpenter and, with his wife, started several businesses in Durango and elsewhere.

Description:
Terrorism--Prevention--2002 Endangered species--Law and legislation--United States--2002
Washington, D.C.; About Narrator: No info provided.; Collection:U004 BASEMENT Room 024: Southwest Oral Histor ; Notes:Interview with Senator Campbell. Includes topics on water rights, endangered species, terrorism, ethnic cleansing of Native Americans, sovereignty, land rights, and education. Wolff and Campbell discuss the expansive nature of the federal government in the wake of the 9/11 terrorist attacks, the need for extra security at the winter Olympics hosted at Salt Lake City, the rights of detainees at Guantanamo Bay, Cuba, and the need for a department of Homeland Defense. Native American issues are also discussed in the second half, including: the history of ethnic cleansing and genocide, Campbell citing statistical data (e.g. unemployment figures) on reservations being in wild excess of what the country at large would tolerate, the nature of tribal sovereignty relative to the federal goverment, federal mismangement of the Native American trust funds (Cobell v. Salazar), historical context on how Native people were dispossesed of their lands, and the educational opportunities available to Native Americans living on reservations. ;

Description:

Washington, D.C.; About Narrator: No info provided.; Collection:U004 BASEMENT Room 024: Southwest Oral Histor ; Notes:Short footage of interview with Senator Campbell support of mounted Capitol Police on horseback at the grounds of the White House. Others in Congress lead by Rep. James. P Moran argued against keeping the mounted unit as too costly to set up a new stable in downtown D.C. and that it duplicated the horseback police units that the U.S. Park Police and the D.C. Police already employed. Campbell predicts that the oppostion is waiting for him to retire before they try to get rid of the horse unit (which they did the following year when he retired). ;

Description: Indians of North America - Health and hygiene - 1996 Football- Training - Arizona - 1996 Chinle (Ariz.) History; About Narrator: No info provided.; Collection:U004 BASEMENT Room 024: Southwest Oral History ; Notes:Hozho Nahadslii (Return to Harmony or Everything Has Returned to Goodness) a N.F.L. players' football camp on Navajoland. Produced by The Johns Hopkins Center for American Indian and Alaskan Native Health, directed by Paul Santomenna. Robert Redford narrates the video, which features interview segments and video clips of Sen. Campbell, Raymond Reid M.D., Debbie Shore, Bill Shore, Steve Reevis, Thomas Atcity, Vincent Craig, Nick Lowery, Rob Moore, Derrick Kennard, Christian Okoye, Mike Bell, Pellom McDaniels, and Kelly Goodburn. The value of sports for young people is the primary focus of the video, emphasizing a holistic view of health that extends into mental health and spiritual fullfilment. ;

Creator: John Hopkins
Description: Legislators - United States - 2004, Television in politics - 2004, Washington, D.C. ; About Narrator: No info provided.; Collection:U004 BASEMENT Room 024: Southwest Oral History ; Notes:Senator Campbell, Bruce Hill, and John Hopkins on a videoconference inside a U.S. Senate recording studio, with Campbell answering telephone questions on Senate and state issues in regards to his assessment of events on the timelines. Approximately 25 people are on the otherside of the call in Grand Junction, CO. Campbell leaves the videoconference about halfway through, which then switches to local and state political matters (mostly TABOR related funding issues). Campbell talks about the $450 billion federal deficit (and CO demands for federal funds to the tune of $400 million), the energy bill (S.2095 and maybe H.R. 4503), the highway bill (H.R. 3971?), and the "viceral" election year issues of gay marriage, abortion, and gun control. Campbell's constituents are mildly shocked at the recent news of his retirement at the end of his term following health scares. ;

Creator: Andy T. Bjarlow
Description: La Plata County (Colo.)--Government; County commissioners--Biography; Durango (Colo.)--History; About Narrator: A Durango native, Mrs. Brennan was the second woman in history to serve as a La Plata County Commissioner (from Jan. 1985 until 1992) and the first to be re-elected for a second term. Prior to that, she had been vice chair and then chair of the La Plata County Democratic Party in the 1960s. According to Sally Morrissey's biographical sketch in the 2/2/1997 Durango Herald, the County budget was automated during her tenure, the new airport terminal was built, the courthouse addition was finished and the county jail was constructed. She was born in Durango in 1920 to Edward and Zipporah McDaniel and was married to Micky Brennan in 1942. She and her husband operated the merchant-owned Credit Bureau of Durango for 40 years until 1985 when they sold it. They also published and distributed a weekly bulletin service to businesses that was a compilation of legal activities recorded at the County Courthouse, a service she continued after Micky's death in 1994.; Collection:U004: Southwest Oral History - BOX 3 ; Notes: ;

Ranchers--Colorado--Dolores 
Dolores (Colo.)--History
; About Narrator: Mrs. Stephenson, the granddaughter of German homesteaders near Dolores, was born at home near Dolores in 1933 and attended Fort Lewis when it was in its final years as a junior college at the old fort campus, then finished her college degree at the new campus in Durango. She taught elementary school 1958-1996, most of those years in school district RE-1 in Cortez, Colorado. She was married to Ray E. Stephenson, a rancher, in 1956; they had known each other in high school, and both attended Fort Lewis.; Collection:U004: Southwest Oral History - BOX 33 ; Notes:Pertaining to the Old German Settlement near Dolores, Colorado. The narrator describes growing up in that region in the 1930s and 1940s, describes the since-flooded town of McPhee, tells stories about cattle drives, and other historically significant information. Transcribed by Catherine Conrad at the Center of Southwest Studies (finished on 9/12/97) and edited by Archivist Todd Ellison and sent on that date to the narrator.; Found in Box #34. TA 11/13/2020

; About Narrator: Age 18 at the time of his experience with the CCC in the San Juan Basin; age 84 at the time of his giving the written narrative to the Center.; Collection:U004: Southwest Oral History ; Notes:Typescript of a reminiscence: Morris Grodsky was "a CCC boy in 1940...stationed at a Bureau of Reclamation CCC camp located at the [Vallecito] Dam site. Our assignment was clearing the timber from the valley, prior to the filling of the dam by the blocking of the Los Pinos river. I have written a little account of that brief period in my life. And as it really is part of the history of the dam and the lake it created, I thought that you might have an interest in it. This account exists as a contribution to a CCC site which is on the internet. It also appears in a book which I have written.";

Description: College students--Colorado--Durango--Biography, Universities and colleges - Colorado - Durango, Durango (Colo.) - History; About Narrator: Dr. Heidy came to Fort Lewis in 1955 as one of the first night students on the new campus on the mesa, then returned on 7/15/1962 as a staff member in various administrative roles including admissions and student life for 8 years prior to taking 12 months' sabbatical and 3 months' leave to obtain his doctorate in psychology at the University of Northern Colorado in 1969/70. He then returned to Fort Lewis College as a full-time faculty member in the Department of the sole professor of psychology. He was active on a number of College committees and programs as a faculty member, and as an administrator in the earlier period he pioneered the college's new student advising program, among other innovations.; Collection:U001: FLC Oral Histories - BOX 37 ; Notes:Interview conducted in President Emeritus John Reed's former office in Reed Library 22. The audiotape ended shortly before the natural conclusion of the interview, but Dr. Heidy needed to be on his way. We discussed the possibility of continuing this interview at some future date, as it seems Dr. Heidy has much to share. Tape given to Catherine Conrad on 7/14/00 to transcribe, which she finished in August; on 9/14/00 Todd Ellison edited and formatted it and mailed a copy to Dr. Heidy for his review.; Found in Box #37. Creator: Todd Ellison
Description:
Electrical power generation and transmission--Colorado--San Juan Mountains--History 
Durango (Colo.)--History; About Narrator: Daughter of P. C. (Philip Chester) Schools who was the son of Nicholas Schools. Both men worked for the Western Colorado Power Company on the Western Slope of Colorado. Her father's work as an electrical engineer was based in Durango, where the family was raised.; Collection:U004: Southwest Oral History - BOX 36 ; Notes:Interview conducted the week after an Elderhostel service project at the Center of Southwest Studies in which the narrator participated. Her friend and Elderhostel teammate Elizabeth Blake quietly listened in on this interview. Toward the end of the interview, the interviewer asked Mrs. Case to explain briefly her role in developing the Research Triangle in North Carolina starting in about 1950, which was a significant contribution of her life, even though that was outside the scope of the Center of Southwest Studies. This audiotaped presentation was transcribed by Brookes Boswell, student archival assistant at the Center of Southwest Studies, in March-April, 2000, and was formatted by Center Archivist Todd Ellison on April 7, 2000 and a copy mailed to Mrs. Case on May 4, 2000. Mrs. Case returned the transcript, along with copies of five letters of reminiscence dated 1967 from her dad to herself and her sister, in June of 2000 with corrections and amplifications [the latter are noted in square brackets] which Ellison input on June 28, 2000, mailing a copy of the revised transcript to the narrator on that date.; Found in Box #36. TA 11/13/2020; MP3 is available online

Creator: Jack Turner
Description: Amador Tucson is a descendent member of the Southern Ute Tribe. He’s the founder, musician, and singer for the Latino band, “Tucson y Los Amigos.” Amador is best known as the owner / operator of Tucson’s Barber & Styling (est. 1968) where he and his son Isador provide classic haircuts today.

Creator: Jack Turner
Topic_Tags: Native American, Arts, Government, Education & Schools, Sports, Military & Veterans
Description: Ben Nighthorse Campbell is an American Cheyenne politician who represented Colorado in the United States House of Representatives and Senate. He was the first Native American elected U.S. Senator. During his time in office, he was the only Native American in Congress. From humble beginnings in poverty and a broken home, Ben Nighthorse Campbell lived an incredible life that encompasses heavy labor, military service, school teaching, the Olympic Games, truck driving, jewelry making, extraordinary public service, and more stories shown in this video with his wife, Linda, and daughter, Shanan.

Creator: Jack Turner
Description: The Lechner family immigrated from Austria to SW Colorado in the late 1800's. Their extended family established ranches in the high alpine valleys north of Durango, CO where Electra Lake, Haviland Lake, and Rockwood are today. This is a fascinating story of pioneer family and generations of local history.

Creator: Jack Turner
Topic_Tags: News & Media, Local Business, Hunting, Fish & Fishing
Description: Prescott Blake moved to Durango in 1933 at age two when his father was hired as a linotype operator for the Durango Democrat newspaper. Prescott is a colorful jack-of-all-trades, hunter, fishman, and owned a local plumbing company for 30 years in addition to working with other contractors on major projects.

Creator: Durango Herald
Description: Local Ski History – Jack Turner is a 5th generation Durango local who learned to ski on Coal Bank Pass, which was popular for premier powder skiing before Purgatory Ski Resort was built. On Purgatory’s opening day in 1964, Jack and his family were there with other excited skiers, most of whom had never ridden a chairlift. Countless hours were spent at Hesperus and Third Avenue Ski Area (the original name locals used for Chapman Hill). Jack competed on the Durango High School Ski Team, was a 6-time NCAA champion and All-American at the University of Colorado, and a member of the U.S. Ski Team. He served as ABC Television’s expert commentator for Nordic skiing at the 1984 Olympic Winter Games and was the first person kicked off the Salt Lake Olympic Committee.

Creator: Jack Turner
Description: Mary Jane Clark is a founder of Toh-Atin Gallery (est. 1957) focusing on Native American and Southwestern artworks, jewelry, and rugs. Mary Jane was born at home in Blanco, NM (about 40 miles from Durango) where her parents owned the Blanco Mercantile trading post. Mary Jane lived in Durango in 1944, as a nursing student, and settled here permanently, working at Mercy Hospital, in 1945.

Creator: Jack Turner
Description: The daughter of two homesteading families, Peryl Kelley eloped and married another generational local, Olga Little’s nephew, Ernie Schaaf, at age 15. Peryl’s work career started at age six as a homemaker for her family while her mother was away teaching at remote one room school houses. Peryl transitioned to a gas pump attendant at her father’s station during the war and eventually served as a Bank of Durango vice-president.

Creator: Jack Turner
Topic_Tags: Education & Schools, Forestry, Sports, Military & Veterans
Description: Rey & Clair Martinez are brothers, two of seven children born at home in Durango. Both men played football for Durango High School and then at Adams State College. Ray served in the Marine Corps and Clair was a Forest Service interpreter. Both brothers taught in the local schools and coached sports for decades.

Creator: Jack Turner
Description: Rod Turner’s great grandfather, John C. Turner, was a member of the Baker Expedition that first explored the area in 1860-61. Six generations of the Turner family are from Durango. Assisted by his daughter, Laurel, Rod’s stories are told with his unique perspective and sense of humor.

Creator: Jack Turner
Topic_Tags: Agriculture & Farming, Education & Schools, Pioneer, Ranching
Description: Tom & Penni Compton focused their lives on family, agriculture, and education. Penni was born into the Lemon family, pioneer ranchers and the namesake for Lemon Reservoir. In addition to operating their ranch in the Hesperus area, Tom taught biology at Fort Lewis College and served many community organizations including 9R School Board, LaPlata Open Space Conservancy and helped start Leadership LaPlata.

Creator: Rural Video Access
Location_Tags: Durango, La Plata County, Mancos, Montezuma County
Description: "A Sense of Place" - A SENSE OF PLACE is a production of Rural Video Access which mobilized hundreds of students and volunteers to collect over 120 hours of oral history interviews with community elders throughtout southwestern Colorado." The first video segment, "Memories of the Mancos Opera House," is 7 minutes long and features Fern Ellis recounting stories of the community opera house which span the decades of World War I through World War II. The second video segment on this casset, "Memories of the Durango Power Plant," is 30 minutes in length and documents the hisory of electricity in Durango and the surrounding agricultural communities in southwestern Colorado.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of an interview of A.J. Chitwood, an early day resident of La Plata County, Colorado. Born in Missouri in 1860, Chitwood's family lived briefly in Fredonia, Kansas before moving to southwestern Colorado where his father established a ranch near present-day Lake City. Chitwood worked for many years as a cowboy in the region.

Creator: CWA
Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of an interview of Mrs. A.P. Camp, an early day resident of La Plata County, Colorado. Camp came to Durango, Colorado in 1883 with her husband and family. Almost all of this interview consists of her recollections of early-day Durango, including a visit by Colorado senator Henry M. Teller as well as an amusing story of a traveling circus and the impression the elephants in the circus made on the Ute Indians who saw them. She also talks about a mobile jail cell, where prisoners were transported en masse on wheels from one location in town to another, and that the sheriff of Durango, Bob Dwyer, would often farm his land with a rifle strapped to the plow handles.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of an interview concerning O.D. Pyle, an early day resident of Montezuma County, Colorado. Born in Ohio in 1853, Pyle married Flora Wilkinson at an unknown date and came west to Colorado, working first at Canyon City and later moving on to Pagosa Springs. The family then moved to New Mexico, but trouble between whites and the Navajos forced them to return to Colorado, where they eventually settled in present-day Montezuma County. For many years Pyle owned a cattle ranch and helped to organized the first schools in the county.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of an interview of Al Bernard, an early day resident of La Plata County, Colorado. Born in Canada in 1851, Bernard came to Colorado in 1872, where he made his way to the southwestern portion of the state with his brother Jim. Much of this interview consists of his recollections of being a freighter to many of the mining camps near Animas City, Colorado during the 1880s. He describes some early colorful characters of the region, including a woman nicknamed "Sage Hen," and also describes being caught in an avalanche and being nursed back to health by some friends who rubbed whisky and cayenne pepper over his body over the course of the following week.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of an interview of Alex Calhoun, an early day resident of Delta County, Colorado. Born in Morrisburg, Ontario in 1854, Calhoun came to Silverton, Colorado in 1878. In 1883 he went into partnership with George West and purchased a large herd of cattle which they pastured in the region of Dolores, Colorado. In the 1890s he operated a meat market in Telluride, Colorado. A portion of this document consists of his recollections of the shooting of a private detective in the early 1880s who had come in to the Dolores region to attempt to capture an unidentified outlaw operating in the region. He also tells about the miners' union in Telluride during the 1890s.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of a biographical sketch of Amos Augustine Frost, an early day resident of western Colorado. Born in West Virginia in 1852, Frost came to Colorado in 1865. Shortly after arriving in Colorado, Frost went north to Wyoming, working first on a rail line and then as a freighter. After a violent encounter with a group of Cheyennes Indians, Frost went back to Colorado and worked as a cowboy, for a time, before heading further west to Nevada and then to the Sacramento Valley of California. He then traveled to Leadville, Colorado, where he worked in a number of mines. After marrying in 1880, he and his wife Nettie settled in the Uncompahgre Valley of western Colorado, settling on a farm near the town of Montrose.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of an interview of Charles Naeglin, an early day resident of La Plata County, Colorado. Born in 1852 in Missouri, Naeglin came to Granada, Colorado in 1873 with his brother Bill. After a short stay, the two opened a blacksmith shop in Del Norte. By 1878 they had moved on to Parrot City in La Plata County, where they again worked as blacksmiths and prospectors. After putting a claim on the North Star Mine, Naeglin sold his portion in 1877 for $80,000, and returned to Missouri where he married. Most of this interview consists of his recollections of the hardships involved in moving in to the county in the 1870s, including a brawl he was drawn into in Cumbres on his return following his marriage in Missouri by a pair of drunken toughs.

A portion of this interview consists of his recollections of the "Bloody Benders", a notorious family of thieves and serial killers who operated near present-day Cherry Vale, Kansas in the early 1870s.

Born in Illinois in 1852, Morgan came to Colorado in 1873, staying first in Denver and then moving to Gunnison County, where he hunted and worked as a freighter for a time. He later operated a ranch and an apple orchard for a number of years in Montezuma County. A portion of this document consists of his recollections of how Tom Tobin, the famous mountain man, tracked down and killed the outlaws the Espinosa brothers in the San Luis Valley in 1863.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of an interview of Harry Cook, an early day resident of Delta County, Colorado. Born in Ontario in 1864, Cook came to southeastern Kansas with his family in 1867. In the late 1880s he moved to the Black Hills region where he prospected and worked as a miner. In 1892 he moved to Creede, Colorado where he operated a boarding house. In 1894 he moved to Telluride. Shortly after he was contracted by Thomas Walsh to help operate the Camp Bird Mine near Ouray. Most of this document consists of his recollections of working for Walsh in Ouray, Telluride, and Silverton.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of a long interview of J.H. Taylor, an early day resident of the Montezuma Valley of Colorado. Born in 1860 in Missouri, Taylor came to Colorado in 1882, finding work as a cowboy on a ranch near the town of Dolores. After marrying, he and his wife established a homestead and orchard in the region. Taylor recounts a number of fights with Ute Indians that took place during the 1880s, including an extended battle between a number of cowboys and a band of Ute Indians led by the chief Mariana in 1881. This battle took place over a number of days and ranged between southwestern Colorado and southeastern Utah.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of an interview concerning J.M. Rush, an early day resident of Mancos, Colorado. Born in 1819 in Georgia, his family moved to Texas when he was a child and as a young adult Rush joined the Texas Rangers. Joining the Confederate Army as a volunteer, he spent most of the way hauling cotton from Texas to Mexico to be shipped world-wide. In 1866 he and his family moved to Oregon, and later California. In 1879 they moved to the Mancos region of Colorado, where he leased a ranch. He later operated a mercantile store in New Mexico and died at the age of 98.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of a humorous account of John Roper, an early day resident of western Colorado, scared a group of Indians whom he identifies as "Navajoes" but who appear to have been Utes, into fleeing onto the Ute Indian Reservation on the border of Colorado and Utah in 1887 during a hunting expedition. Roper had a buckskin coat with buttons of the type worn by officials known to the Utes as "Buckskin Police", and this story recounts how the Utes mistook him for a policeman and fled back to their reservation because they were denied hunting rights in the region at the time.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of a biographical sketch of Peter Guillet, an early day resident of Cortez, Colorado. Born in Missouri in 1855, Guillet's family were slaveholders and fled to Brazil during the Civil War in an attempt to establish a coffee plantation. Finding the climate unhealthy, they came back to the United States in 1866. As a young man he went to California, and worked for a time as a streetcar conductor in San Francisco. In 1882 he came to Colorado, where he and his brother Herman operated a trading post near Durango. In 1890 he came to Cortez with his brother and the two operated a flour mill for many years.

Much of this interview consists of her recollections of her dealings with the Ute Indians of the region, who had great respect for her bravery and affectionately called her "Bueno."

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of an interview of Sarah Moore, an early day resident of Dolores, Colorado. Born in Iowa in 1859, Moore came to Colorado in 1880, living for a time with her brother at Robinson's Camp, a short-lived mining camp near present-day Kokomo in the Arkansas Valley. In 1881 she married George Moore. The couple settled near Gunnison, but the high altitude proved difficult for Sarah and she moved to Langton, Kansas to live with a sister while her husband worked to save up money to buy land near Dolores, Colorado for a ranch. A portion of this interview consists of her recollections of the fear in the region up to and following the killing of John Genthner by Ute Indians in 1885.

Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of a biographical sketch of Sterling Sherman, an early day resident of western Colorado. Born in New York in 1852, Sherman was educated in Germany as an engineer. In 1875 he returned to the United States, and unable to find work in his profession he moved back to New York and studied to be a lawyer. After passing the bar, he came to Lake City, Colorado in 1879, where he eventually established a law practice. However, Lake City suffered from an economic downturn and he moved to Delta, Colorado, where he again worked as a lawyer. He later moved to Montrose, working again as a lawyer. In later years his son Edward joined him in his legal practice.

In the next 5 years he alternated work as a freighter and in mining. In 1911 he came to Delta County, where he worked as a day laborer building irrigation canals, ditches, and highway. Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of a biographical sketch of Virdie Hotchkiss, an early day resident of western Colorado. Creator: CWA
Location_Tags: Ignacio, La Plata County, Durango, Silverton, San Juan County, Leadville, Lake County, Southern Ute Reservation
Description: General Note: Part of the CWA Pioneer Interviews Collection. Consists of an interview of William Dobbins, an early day resident of La Plata County, Colorado. Born in Donegal County in Ireland, Dobbins came to Leadville, Colorado in 1879. In 1881 he traveled to southwestern Colorado as part of a group of prospectors, but returned to Leadville without success. Later in that year he traveled to Ignacio, and then on to Durango. He went on to work as a freighter and miner in the region. In a portion of this interview he discusses the mining magnate Horace Tabor's financial interests in the region he also talks about the frightening experience of being caught in an avalanche and almost dying in the aftermath.

Creator: Adeline Plantz Wasem
Description: Addie Wasem, daughter of George Parker Plantz and Ellen "Nell" Chivers Plantz, and granddaughter of Dr. Timothy Parker Plantz. Involves information requested about the Plantz residences on Empire Street at the north end of Silverton, some recollections of the neighborhood, including John Meager's molestation of his daughters, and the Silverton Northern Railroad.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Agatha was a long time resident of San Juan County, recalls assorted aspects of her life in San Juan County and discusses business locations and other civic Silverton community history. Interview was by Allen Nossaman at Agatha's Denver residence, 1470 Ivy Street.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Interview with Silverton native and long time resident (1914-1999). Covers childhood in Silverton, Silverton characters and his career as mill man and superintendent at Shenandoah (Mayflower) Mill.

Narrator: Alfa Lyons; Robert Shock; Jack Rentfrow
Creator: Gerald Swanson
Description: ACROSS THE GREAT DIVIDE – RAILROADING IN THE SAN JUAN. Participants were retired employees of the D&RGW RR. The discussed their experiences with the railroad and contemporaries. 3 part recording. "Across the Great Divide" was planned in 1976 by Irene Epp, then Director of the Silverton Public Library, The San Juan County Commissioners, the San Juan County Historical Society, and San Juan Regional Planning Commission. The series of nine weekly programs was designed to promote discussion regarding the planning of future development in San Juan County. The first four meeting were concerned with history of the county during its first century. Sessions five thru eight focused on current issues. The final session was a summing up of the issues addressed throughout the county. Each program involved local residents, humanities scholars and technical experts.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Location_Tags: Silverton, San Juan County, Mesa County, Grand Junction
Description: Patterson was 92 years old at the time of this interview. Patterson was in the San Juans from approximately 1919 through 1928. He leased the Joker Tunnel to intersect the Guston vein, worked at the Sunnyside Mine and was a contractor on the adaptation of the Million Dollar Highway to an automobile road during the early 1920s. He ultimately became a foreman for the U.S. Bureau of Roads as the federal road system expanded. Occasional female voice on the tape was Patter

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Location_Tags: Silverton, Durango, San Juan County, La Plata County
Description: Alva Lyons was a long-time railroad conductor with the D&RGW RR on the Silverton branch who fought the company's attempts to abandon the line. Covers his family history, career with the railroad and recollections of earlier days in Silverton and Durango.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Location_Tags: Silverton, San Juan County, Durango, La Plata County
Description: Charles Wing was born in Durango in 1898 and worked for Western Colorado Power Company many years. Ruth Rathmell Wing wrote a book and several articles on history of the San Juans. Charles was 82 years old and almost blind at the time of this interview. He discusses recollections of stories told him regarding early Silverton history. Includes a geographic progression through many of the mines and mills in the northern and eastern portions of San Juan County. Mrs. Wing contributes much background data and verification during the discussion. Multiple part recording

Creator: Allen Nossaman and Gerald Swanson
Location_Tags: Montrose County, Montrose, Silverton, San Juan County
Description: In the kitchen of Swanson's Market on Blair Street in Silverton. Charlie was the man who drove the mail truck from Montrose to Silverton over Red Mountain Pass during the period February 1950 to March 1980 - 30 years. This interview was during the week Charlie was retiring as mail carrier for the U.S. Post Office. He discusses his experiences during his 30 year career, with notable, and understandable, emphasis on winter driving experiences on the sometimes treacherous road.

Narrator: Dagmar Quarnstrom; Irma Quarnstrom Cleavinger; Myrtle Sutherland Stefan
Creator: Helen Nossaman
Topic_Tags: Education & Schools, Medicine, Youth, Daily Life
Description: Interview by Helen Nossaman. Dagmar, born 1906, and Irma, born 1907, were the daughters of Louis and Edla Svedenborg Quarnstrom. Myrtle Sutherland Stefan, born 1906, was the daughter of Matthew and Annie Stromberg Sutherland. All three women, Dagmar, Irma and Myrtle, were born in Silverton. They discuss their childhood, families, school, Otto Mears, nursing and doctors, as well as other subjects.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Interview includes recollections and notes regarding her husband's remembrances of Silverton. Her husband, Arthur Hilding Berquist, the son of Gus and Hilda Swanson Berquist, was born in Silverton in 1905 and died in 1987. His father, August "Gus" Berquist, came to America in 1890, to Silverton in 1891, and married Hilda Swanson in 1901. Gus was in the saloon business, worked in the mines and died in Silverton 1913. Louise Berquist Christopher was daughter of Della and Arthur Hilding Berquist.

Creator: unknown
Location_Tags: Silverton, San Juan County, Durango, La Plata County
Description: Della (1901-1987) was the daughter of Silverton pioneer Charles Melton. She recalls the early history of her father and her own career as a telephone operator in Silverton. She married Nondo Giacomelli, shoe repairman in Silverton and Durango and assisted in operation of the business.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Eddie was the son of early-day Silverton saloon owner, Italian Counsel and photographer, John V. Lorenzon. This interview covers Lorenzon family history, childhood in Silverton, his father's role as Italian counsel, and as a Silverton merchant and amazing photographer. During the interview, Eddie produced the panoramic camera utilized by his father to make so many of his characteristic exposures, proving that the camera was still in excellent condition.

Creator: unknown
Location_Tags: Silverton, San Juan County, Durango, La Plata County
Description: Edna was the daughter of pioneer ranch and market owner Gus Ambold and his wife, Lottie Spietz. She reminisces about the Gustavus and Robert Ambold families and their roles in early-day Silverton and Durango.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Fenrick was a Silverton native and member of dance bands formed in the community since 1918. He discusses a wide range of musical subjects, including musicians in the county, and his involvement with dance bands and marching bands through the years: Sydney Six, Sunnyside Seven, Silverton Serenaders. Dates covered: 1918-1950s. 2 part recording

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Covers assorted recollections of Silverton. Includes some data on building and business locations. This interview was shortly before Fenrick's death on April 17, 1984.

Narrator: Ernest "Tom" Zanoni; Ernest Hoffman; Louis Dalla; "Corky" Scheer; Fenrick Sutherland; George L. Zanoni
Creator: Ernest Thomas "Tom" Zanoni
Description: ACROSS THE GREAT DIVIDE - MINING IN THE SAN JUANS. Participants discuss individual involvements with mining industry in San Juan County. Recollections of humorous and significant stories, discoveries and achievements. Also humorous accounts of life as Silverton kids. "Across the Great Divide" was planned in 1976 by Irene Epp, then Director of the Silverton Public Library, The San Juan County Commissioners, the San Juan County Historical Society, and San Juan Regional Planning Commission. The series of nine weekly programs was designed to promote discussion regarding the planning of future development in San Juan County. The first four meeting were concerned with history of the county during its first century. Sessions five thru eight focused on current issues. The final session was a summing up of the issues addressed throughout the county. Each program involved local residents, humanities scholars and technical experts. 2 part recording.

Creator: Ted Foster
Description: RADIO STATION SERIES. Interview of Ernest Hoffman and George L. Zanoni on mining history, red light district, ethnic groups and general area history by Ted Foster, Vice President and general manager of Radio San Juan, Inc. (KIUP/KRSJ). Includes commercial messages for sponsors.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: "Tug" accompanied his father, David L. Mechling, in the first automobile, a Croxton- Keaton, to enter Silverton, which occurred in the summer of 1910. He recalls that first auto trip over Stony Pass, as well as other stories of his father, a pioneer pharmacist Silverton. David Mechling lived to more than 100 years. Reminiscences of the Wyman family are also included.

Narrator: Frances Beaber; James R. Bell; E. Fenrick Sutherland; Mary Dalla Swanson; Gerald Swanson; Leonard "Red" Bird
Creator: Gerald Swanson
Description: ACROSS THE GREAT DIVIDE-GROWING UP IN SILVERTON. Participants discuss various aspects of life as children and young adults in Silverton and compare views on quality of life as Silverton residents. "Across the Great Divide" was planned in 1976 by Irene Epp, then Director of the Silverton Public Library, The San Juan County Commissioners, the San Juan County Historical Society, and San Juan Regional Planning Commission. The series of nine weekly programs was designed to promote discussion regarding the planning of future development in San Juan County. The first four meeting were concerned with history of the county during its first century. Sessions five thru eight focused on current issues. The final session was a summing up of the issues addressed throughout the county. Each program involved local residents, humanities scholars and technical experts.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Corky (1914-1993) was born in Silverton, the son of Charles and Pearl Scheer. He was small of stature but had a reputation of Herculean proportions because of his outrageous, unbelievable, fantastic behavior. He was often pointed out as "the man who eats glass and razor blades". He himself estimated he probably ate glass at least 50 times during his life and razor blades a couple of times. He recalls his family and career, including a stint in the demolition corps in World War II.

Creator: unknown
Description: Interview with former Silverton residents who lived in Silverton between 1940 and the late 1960s. Includes recollections of mining and milling work and of community life and friends. Frank once fell 70 feet from the tram buckets on the Shenandoah (Mayflower) tram line and lived., which is also discussed.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Location_Tags: Silverton, San Juan County, Durango, La Plata County
Description: Fury (1892-1989) reviews his family history, the Iron Mountain Saloon, his business pursuits in Silverton and other aspects of the commercial and political history of the community Fury was born in Michigan and died in Durango. 2 part recording.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Topic_Tags: Education & Schools, Daily Life, Youth, Arts
Description: Fury (1905-1988), son of John and Domenica Todeschi Dalla, was born in Silverton; He was a teacher in the Silverton school system several years, moved to California and spent the rest of his teaching career there. In this interview he recalled growing up in Silverton, family history, reminiscences as a teacher in Silverton and cultural and artistic events in Silverton

Creator: Allen Nossaman and Don Stott
Description: George Landry, former Silverton resident, was one of the first to attempt radio reception in Silverton. He was a descendant of the Landry family which operated the Frog Saloon in Silverton. Covers recollections of growing up in Silverton, school years and numerous experiences as an adult before leaving Silverton.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: George was a Silverton native, son and nephew of operators of the Marsha Rose Smelter, and very instrumental in periodical school reunions for Silverton graduates. He graduated in 1923. He was born July 4, 1903 (died 1985) and recalls family history and stories of growing up, attending school and working in Silverton prior to leaving Silverton as a young man. He was the son of Henry and Carrie Solberg (or Sohlberg) Van Bocken. His father died when he was two years old, and his mother married Ben Boyd, Silverton contractor and master carpenter, in 1910.

Creator: George Van Bocken
Description: Recorded by Van Bocken for submission to George A. Chapman, publisher and editor of The Silverton Standard and the Miner, for the latter's use as material for a weekly column. The material was the basis for "Tales of Tapper Light" which ran in The Standard starting in the fall of 1979.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Plantz was the great-grandson of San Juan County pioneer Timothy Parker Plantz, called Parker, (to Silverton 1875). George was the grandson of long-time Silverton resident, George Parker Plantz and his wife, Ellen "Nell" Chivers Plantz, and was the son of Ralph Albert and Emma Katherine Shaddle Plantz. George Willis, nicknamed "Blackie" was born in Silverton in 1908. He recalls many of his experiences growing up in Silverton and memories of his family.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: "Blackie" was the son of Ralph and Emma Shaddle Plantz, grandson of George Parker and Nell Chivers Plantz and great-grandson of T. Parker Plantz. He includes recollections of the Plantz family and San Juan County history, growing up in Silverton, childhood friends and pranks. He worked at the Sunnyside Mine and Mill for a time.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Location_Tags: Silverton, San Juan County, Delta, Delta County
Description: Interview with Dr. Gerald Burgess, born in Canon City in 1925 and raised in Silverton. He worked as a trucker and garage man and after World War II enrolled in dental school. He lived in Delta, Colorado at the time of this interview.

Creator: Allen Nossaman and Mike Andreatta
Description: Gerald was born in Silverton. His grandfather, John Glanville, came to America from Cornwall in 1879. Gerald graduated from Silverton High School in 1947. He worked as a heavy equipment operator, then as a mine hoist man. He discusses information on photographs, mostly relating to mines, mills, county roads and various individuals. The interview was by Allen Nossaman and Mike Andreatta.

Narrator: Gerald Swanson; James Bell; Fred Andersen
Creator: Gerald Swanson
Description: ACROSS THE GREAT DIVIDE-GROWING UP IN SILVERTON- IN SEARCH OF THE SIGNAL. Participants discuss the long, strenuous and difficult attempts to bring reliable television reception to Silverton in the late 1950s. Discussion was illustrated with color slides from their respective collections, most taken on Boulder Mountain and Hazelton Ridge. During the search for a reliable signal and the attempt to erect dependable equipment for the relay of the signal, with references to and background remarks from Leon Griner. "Across the Great Divide" was planned in 1976 by Irene Epp, then Director of the Silverton Public Library, The San Juan County Commissioners, the San Juan County Historical Society, and San Juan Regional Planning Commission. The series of nine weekly programs was designed to promote discussion regarding the planning of future development in San Juan County. The first four meeting were concerned with history of the county during its first century. Sessions five thru eight focused on current issues. The final session was a summing up of the issues addressed throughout the county. Each program involved local residents, humanities scholars and technical experts.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Location_Tags: Durango, La Plata County, Silverton, San Juan County, Alamosa, Alamosa County
Description: Bud Schaaf was a Durango native who worked as brakeman, fireman and engineer for the D&RGW RR and its narrow gauge operations on the San Juan Division, from Alamosa to Silverton and to Farmington, New Mexico. Recollections are primarily of family members and railroading experiences during his years on the narrow gauge - roughly from 1959 until 1987. Emphasis on the people with whom he worked and situations which arose in the company and in operations of the lines. He was also a conductor on the Silverton Branch toward the end of his career.

Creator: unknown
Description: Includes information on life in Silverton and on his work as a miner at the Shenandoah- Dives Mine. He was a member of the first crew to break through into the old workings of the Silver Lake.

Creator: Bonnie Jean, Hadden Cole, Boglino Tesch
Description: Hazel played honky-tonk piano at the Bent Elbow Restaurant in Silverton many years. Piano selection recorded by Allen Nossaman at the Bent Elbow on Blair Street.

Creator: Henry Merrill Doud
Description: Self-recorded monologues by Henry M. Doud (1901-1983). Covers family history, role of the Doud family in San Juan County and his career with Western Colorado Power Company. Multiple recordings.

Creator: unknown
Description: Herman T. Heath was the son of Herbert G. and Louise Mayer Heath, who lived in Silverton and Animas Forks between 1906 and 1910. The father worked for The Silverton Standard, the First National Bank of Silverton and the Gold Prince Mine and Mill at Animas Forks. In Animas Forks the family lived in the Duncan bay window house. He discusses the history of the Heath family.

Creator: Jack Rentfro
Location_Tags: Durango, La Plata County, Pueblo, Pueblo County, Silverton, San Juan County
Description: Self Recorded in 1993 and 1994. Rentfro was with the D&RGW RR between 1935 and 1981, and was involved with the narrow gauge starting in about 1940 when he was assigned to Durango from Pueblo. He retired as Trainmaster of the Silverton branch when the railroad was purchased by Charles Bradshaw Jr. in 1981. This series of CDs deals with his railroad career, people, events, changes and experiences over his many years of railroading.

Creator: unknown
Description: Interview with Silverton native, son of Marion and Anna McNutt Bell. This interview was prior to his leaving Silverton for a lower climate due to health

Narrator: James R. Bell; Louis Dalla; Fenrick Sutherland; George L. "Leach" Zanoni; Ernest Thomas Zanoni
Creator: Ernest Thomas "Tom" Zanoni
Description: ACROSS THE GREAT DIVIDE - MINING-OLD TIMERS. Participants discuss their varied involvement with the mineral industry in San Juan County and recall experiences working for mining companies. "Across the Great Divide" was planned in 1976 by Irene Epp, then Director of the Silverton Public Library, The San Juan County Commissioners, the San Juan County Historical Society, and San Juan Regional Planning Commission. The series of nine weekly programs was designed to promote discussion regarding the planning of future development in San Juan County. The first four meeting were concerned with history of the county during its first century. Sessions five thru eight focused on current issues. The final session was a summing up of the issues addressed throughout the county. Each program involved local residents, humanities scholars and technical experts.

Creator: Ted Foster
Description: RADIO STATION SERIES JAMES W. COLE-COMMUNITY. Interview of James W. Cole on community, family and mining by Ted Foster, Vice President and general manager of Radio San Juan, Inc. (KIUP/KRSJ). Includes commercial messages for sponsors.

Narrator: James W. Cole; Louis Dalla; Ernest Hoffman; Allen Nossaman; Gerald Swanson; Ian Thompson
Creator: Ian M. Thompson
Location_Tags: San Juan County, Silverton, Durango, La Plata County
Description: DURANGO CENTENNIAL. Recording of the first of a series of seminar programs sponsored by the Durango Centennial Commission on the history of Durango during the community's centennial years. This program dealt with relationships between Durango and Silverton through the years. 2 part recording.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: At time of this interview, Jim (1916-1982), born in Silverton, was preparing to leave Silverton for a lower elevation. He had been identifying photos before the tape started, and discusses mine and mill memories related to single-piston engines. Also talks of the Silverton Bears Semi-Pro baseball team, Sheepman's Days, Silverton dairies, business locations and other subjects.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Interview with brothers Joe and John Matties, born and raised in Silverton. Their family operated a saloon and rooming house on Blair Street. Included Matties family history, the family business (The Welcome Saloon), as well as various Silverton personalities and businesses.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Joe, retired San Juan County road overseer and road crew member, recounts his experiences, fatalities he recalled, snow and avalanche hazards on county roads and numerous local personalities, including Swede Erickson and Jesse Carey.

Narrator: Joe Todeschi; Teddie Todeschi; Celia Troglia; Marjorie Baudino; Freda Peterson
Creator: Allen Nossaman and Freda Peterson
Topic_Tags: Youth, Daily Life, Medicine, Crime, Automobiles & Roads, Local Business
Description: Those interviewed: Joe and Teddie Todeschi, Celia Troglia, Marjorie Baudino and Freda Peterson. Joe Todeschi and Celia Todeschi Troglia are brother and sister. Joe and Teddie, husband and wife. Teddie Carley Todeschi, Freda Carley Peterson and Marjorie Carley Baudino are sisters. Interview and conversation among participants deals with childhood and family history in Silverton, recollections of Blair Street and bootlegging activities, connection between the families, the hospital in Silverton and its staff through the years, details of the car accident which Teddie Todeschi survived in 1963; she was accompanying Sam and Melva Bland to the hospital in Durango for the birth of their fourth child; Melva and the unborn child (boy) were killed. 4 parts of recording.

Creator: unknown
Description: Interview with John Klements, former Silverton resident. He was the son of John L. Sr. and Mary Gertrude Drobnick Klements. John L. Sr. was the son of John and Anna Klepper Klements (later Slabonick). Includes genealogical information on the complex Klements, Slabonick, Kolar, Savich and Drobnick families, and material relating to identification of photos.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Interview with John McNamara, long-time resident of the San Juan Basin. Born in Eureka, he remembers growing up, the layout of the former town, family history and his career.

Creator: Allen Nossaman and Andrew Hanahan
Description: John lived in Silverton during the 1960s and 1970s. He taught at the school and was director of summer melodrama in Silverton. He discusses melodrama during the summer seasons, great attention to personnel by years, and to his role as director. He was part- owner of the San Juan Bar and Melodrama on Greene Street for some of those years.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Julia was the daughter of Constantino August “Gus” and Letizia Cardini Maffei, founders of the French Bakery, a grocery and market. Julia was their only child and she traces the history of the French Bakery to the eventual closing in the early 1970s. Also includes recollections of family history and Silverton businesses.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: At different times Laura was married to Blair Street musicians (accordions). Her first husband, Ray Lindoffer, wanted her to "go on the line” in Silverton. Her second husband was Joe Pachek, who, besides playing the accordion, played baseball for Silverton. She discusses her associations, and friendships with many Blair Street prostitutes, gamblers, pimps and saloon keepers.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Location_Tags: Silverton, San Juan County, San Miguel County, Telluride
Description: Interview with 90 year old Lew Parcell (1888-1990), long-time Silverton resident, merchant and political party chairman. Dates covered are generally 1890s thru 1930s. He discusses his life before coming to Colorado. He first went to Telluride, then to Silverton as a railroad telegrapher. Also talks of his early interests in Silverton, the Circle Route Stage, which later evolved into the Circle Route Garage.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Location_Tags: Silverton, San Juan County, San Miguel County, Telluride
Description: Parcell was a long-time Silverton resident, merchant and political party chairman. He was 94 years old at the time of this interview; another interview in 1979 is a separate CD. He resumes recollections made in an earlier interview (F-5-E-2-16), and discusses the Circle Route Garage, Silverton Light Company and Grand Imperial Hotel.

Creator: Lila Scheer
Description: He recounts personal experiences of his very colorful life, including tattoos covering his body, and career. After leaving Silverton he was often called "Mickey"- that name was tattooed on his hand - that was the nickname of his first wife (of many wives), Marjorie Dell "Mickey" Garrison (later Estes).

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: He was born in Silverton in 1910. His father was a plumber, pipe fitter and inventor. His siblings were Frank "Corky", Lena (married Bausman), Pearl (married O'Malley). He recalls his personal experiences growing up in Silverton and various aspects of his family's history and involvement with Silverton.

Creator: unknown
Description: Lillian was 87 years old at the time of this interview. She moved to Silverton in 1897 and taught school in Silverton between 1919 and 1932. She discusses family history, Silverton, growing up and teaching in Silverton. References are made to her brother, Charles H. Gardner, who raised her after her father's death. Lillian's husband, Thomas E. Higgins, died in 1975.

Creator: unknown
Description: Interview with Louis W. "Louie" Dalla, born in Silverton in 1908 (died 1982). He discusses his recollections as a State Highway Department employee in San Juan County. He worked a total of 34 years with county and state highway crews in San Juan County. He went to work for the state in 1936. Louie methodically names the major avalanches along U.S. 550, both north and south of Silverton. He discusses the origin of their names, their characteristics and his hair-raising experiences in clearing them. His memory for dates of specific happenings is accurate. Covers the period 1930 - 1978.

Creator: unknown
Description: This interview was shortly before the couple's 50th wedding anniversary. Both were born in Silverton, Louie in 1908 (died 1982) and Lena in 1912 (died 1999). They recall their marriage in Aztec, New Mexico and various aspects of their lives together, as well as Silverton happenings from 1931 until 1981.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Interview with Margaret Fedrizzi Young, long-time Silverton resident, wife of Roy Young. She was daughter of Alfred and Ida Elvira Bassetti Bonavida Fedrizzi, who were married in 1917. Other children in the family were Alcide, Eugenia and Livia Fedrizzi, and (half-brothers and sister of Margaret) Akdo, Albino "Beans", Henry "Chico" and Lida Bonavida. Roy Young worked with many of the old Italian miners, and Margaret did laundry for some of the men. Margaret discusses family life and identifies some photos belonging to the late Ben Bagozzi, and identifies characteristics of many Italian miners and town residents, as well as some of the customs and influences of the Italian community. Several other voices are noted on the recordings, and include Roy Young, their daughter, Gail Young Zarkis, nephew Ron Distel and grandson John Paul Zarkis.

Creator: unknown
Location_Tags: Silverton, Durango, La Plata County, San Juan County
Description: Interview with Marvin and Ruth, regional historians and historic photograph collectors. Ruth was a member of the Shaw family which settled early day Animas City, and involved with packing and other business activities in early Silverton and Howardsville. Includes recollections of Shaw family history, historic subjects relating to southwestern Colorado and various photographic reproduction and collections. Dates: Generally 1876-1940

Narrator: Mary L. Andersen; Margaret "Marge” Louise Prince Bell; James W. Cole; Gerald Swanson
Creator: Gerald Swanson
Description: ACROSS THE GREAT DIVIDE-GROWING UP IN SILVERTON. Participants from a later generation that earlier programs, discuss their family histories and recollections of growing up in Silverton, and changes they have witnessed in the community. "Across the Great Divide" was planned in 1976 by Irene Epp, then Director of the Silverton Public Library, The San Juan County Commissioners, the San Juan County Historical Society, and San Juan Regional Planning Commission. The series of nine weekly programs was designed to promote discussion regarding the planning of future development in San Juan County. The first four meeting were concerned with history of the county during its first century. Sessions five thru eight focused on current issues. The final session was a summing up of the issues addressed throughout the county. Each program involved local residents, humanities scholars and technical experts.

Creator: unknown
Location_Tags: Silverton, San Juan County, Durango, La Plata County
Description: Interview with Merritt McKenzie, San Juan Basin printing and linotype machinist since 1929. He discusses his mechanic career which began with the Durango Herald-Democrat in 1929. He worked for the merged Durango Herald-News which finally became the Durango Herald, but was the primary source of linotype mechanical trouble-shooting for print shops in southwestern Colorado. He estimated he worked in 38 different shops scattered from Belen, NM to Salida, CO.

Creator: Ted Foster
Description: RADIO STATION SERIES NELLIE JANE BROWN HILL. Interview of Nellie Jane Brown Hill on her family and involvement with mining, by Ted Foster, Vice President and general manager of Radio San Juan, Inc. (KIUP/KRSJ). Includes commercial messages for sponsors.

Creator: unknown
Description: Interview with Nellie Jane Brown Hill, long-time Silverton resident and former town clerk. She graduated from Silverton High School in 1916, then worked as a telephone operator. She married Howard Hill in 1918. This interview covers identification of photos and she discusses various subjects in Silverton history.

Creator: Allen Nossaman and Freda Peterson
Description: Interviewed by Allen Nossaman and Freda Peterson concerning specific questions of Peterson regarding Nellie's related families, including the Brown, Lonergan, Geany, Hill and other families. Emphasis was on genealogical information and recollections of family members. Nellie's daughter, Winifred (Sister Mary Mechtilde) was also involved in the interview.

Creator: Larry Duthie
Description: Interview with former San Juan County Assessor, Norman F. Bawden (1893-1985). He was born in Red Mountain. Dates covered in this interview, generally 1910 - 1950.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Otto Mears Pitcher was the son of James Robertson Pitcher II and Cora Mears Pitcher; Cora was the daughter of Otto and Mary Kampfshulte Mears. Interview by Allen Nossaman and other San Juan County Historical Society members at a dinner given in Pitcher's honor at the home of Gene and Mickie Halaburt in Silverton. F-5-E-7-5 is Pitcher's public address at Silverton Town Hall as part of a program staged in the San Juan County Historical Society in recognition of the National Narrow Gauge Convention in Southwestern Colorado.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Location_Tags: San Juan County, Silverton, Durango, La Plata County
Description: Pete Ferrari was born in Silverton in 1917; his mother skied from Gladstone to Silverton to give birth to him. Pete's father died the next year in the 1918 flu epidemic. Pete discusses his family background and ancestors, his mother's life and remarriages and his own memories of Blair Street businesses and personalities. The family moved to Durango in 1927.

Narrator: Rena Dalla Antonelli; Nellie Jane Brown Hill; Fenrick Sutherland; Mary Dalla Swanson; Norma Crawford Wyman; Gerald Swanson
Creator: Gerald Swanson
Description: ACROSS THE GREAT DIVIDE-GROWING UP IN SILVERTON. Participants discuss various aspects of life as children and young adults in Silverton. 2 part recording. "Across the Great Divide" was planned in 1976 by Irene Epp, then Director of the Silverton Public Library, The San Juan County Commissioners, the San Juan County Historical Society, and San Juan Regional Planning Commission. The series of nine weekly programs was designed to promote discussion regarding the planning of future development in San Juan County. The first four meeting were concerned with history of the county during its first century. Sessions five thru eight focused on current issues. The final session was a summing up of the issues addressed throughout the county. Each program involved local residents, humanities scholars and technical experts.

Creator: Allen Nossaman
Description: Warren and Byron Pyle were brothers and were grand- nephews of Bert Wilkinson, who was lynched in Silverton in 1881. Their aunt was Alice Kimball, one-time County Assessor in Silverton, and half-sister of Flora (Mrs. Orville) Pyle. This interview followed a field trip to the site of Bert Wilkinson's burial place. After Bert's lynching he had been buried by Flora and Orville Pyle. In 1987 the site was the edge of the southern end of the paved parking lot of the Silver Pick condominium development just north of Hotter's ranch on Highway 550. Interview deals with Wilkinson background, as known by the family, the Stockton-Eskridge outlaw gang, and some remarks on Alice Kimball.

Description: Born in Silverton, Mickey was the son of William Davidson Logan, born in Aberdeen, Scotland, and the former Mary Isabella Mitchell, born in Springfield, MA. His grandparents were David and Agnes Mathieson Logan. Mickey graduated from Silverton High School in 1924. He and Lillian Tiffany were married in 1947 and lived many years in Durango where Mickey worked as an electrician. He is buried in Durango. He had 3 brothers, Alva George 1909-1926; Lincoln Lawrence 1907-1930; and an infant brother who died in 1906. Interview includes growing up in Silverton, genealogy, Silverton Northern Railroad, early Silverton characters and his business career.

Description: An oral history interview, which took place on August 12, 1992, between Davine Pera and Grace and Harold Brewer. The length is about an hour and a half. Here is the transcription: Brewer, Harold & Grace OH78 1625 Dover Rd, Montrose, CO 81401 Aug. 12, 1992 Davine Pera 728-3632 CD1 42 tracks 47:17 CD2 31 tracks 47:14 CD1 T1 Intro T2 History of Grace's parents. Father came from England to Ophir to work in the mines. He met his mother there, who was from Black Hawk, CO, and she was working in uncle's saloon at Ophir. Father was road overseer. Grace was born in Ophir. T3 Describes attending school in Ophir Loop. There were 3 schools in area, one at Old Ophir, one at Ophir Loop and one at the Alta Mine. T4 Describes winters in Ophir. Speaks of skiing & coasting. She walked to school, about 1/4 mile. T5 Describes clothes they wore. Mother made most of the family’s clothes. T6 Speaks of her brother's death. She had six sisters and brothers. T7 Talks about her mother's hard life. She walked to Ames power plant to work, every day, before she was married. T8 Father worked at mines, but mostly worked as road overseer. Describes his work. T9 Speaks of Magnolia Snow Slide, which was too big to clear out of road, so they tunneled through it. It was over a mile long. T10 Tells the various places she lived. Talks about working at her sister's store and post office in Ophir Loop, when she was a young adult. T11 Names businesses in Ophir Loop. Mentions riding the train to town (Telluride) on weekends to see her mother. Silver Bell. T12 Grace's parents owned a dance hall in Old Ophir. Mother sold sandwiches and coffee. T13 Local people provided music. People from Telluride, Rico, etc. came over to dance. T14 Lots of card parties at different houses. T15 She picked lots of raspberries in the summer. She walked from Old Ophir to Trout Lake and back to fish. T16 There was a small community at Matterhorn. Rosseta Gallo. Beslax. Describes trip to Trout Lake when the bridge at Matterhorn broke down. T17 Picked mushrooms with Mrs. Clementi. Speaks of various families that lived in Ophir. Hawkins. Pengrazi. T18 Speaks of Mattri Clementi's death at Christmas. T19 Grace moved to Telluride in 1935 from Ophir. She talks of her first husband. T20 She worked at the American Legion Hospital after she was married. Dr. Parker. Dora Woods. T21 Names various places she worked before going to work for Troy and Ione Rose at the grocery store. Buckley. I. Wichman. She worked at the grocery store for 25 years. T22 Harold talks about the reasons he moved to Colorado from Iowa. Sold his farm, moved mother and kids into town and started traveling west. T23 Moved to Fort Collins where he worked for a painting contractor until the work ran out during the depression, then later worked at the sugar factory during the sugar campaign. T24 When that closed, he tried placer mining. T25 Describes a sugar campaign. Later he came to Naturita and worked his way to Placerville, gold panning. Cassidy. T26 Judge Dill suggested he go to work at the mine. Cashed his gold in at the drug store (Busy Pharmacy) with Frank Wilson. T27 Describes working at the mine for Ben Grimes. Worked for $4 a day for back pay, including room and board. Cy Washer. Telluride Mine. T28 He helped the electricians repair the power plant at the Red Mill. He operated the power plant for 12 hours a day during the summer, then worked at the Pennsylvania Tunnel during the winter, running the tram. T29 Describes his work. T30 It took 2 years to get the mine and power plant into operation. The men had to sue Grimes to collect their back pay and settled for $2 a day. R Ferguson. T31 Describes the fire at the Pennsylvania Tunnel. Had to rebuild all the buildings there. No men were killed in the fire, as they escaped up the back shaft and came out the Bullion Tunnel. T32 After the mine went back into operation, he worked in the warehouse. Anderson. T33 Operated the warehouse from 1933 until 1954. T34 Idarado bought the mine and combined the operation with the Ouray side. T35 He was laid off so he worked at the Four Corners Uranium in Bull Canyon. Worked there 1 year, then moved to Ophir to work at the Silver Bell. Al Smith. T36 After that, he worked at the County Assessor's office for a year. H. Ballard. T37 He met Grace at dinner at Charles Fanchers. T38 They were married in 1941. Harold raised Francis as his own son. T39 Grace speaks of close relationship with Ann Pullman. T40 They were married in Montrose and went to Iowa for honeymoon. Speak of their son, Francis. T41 He talks about the Red Mill and about the tram at the new mill. T42 Speaks of picture in the Rio Grande Book. End CD1 CD2 T1 Brewers owned Roses Grocery Store from 1957 until 1975. They talk about buying the store from Troy Rose. J. Hardy. T2 Mortgaged car and house for $4000 to stock the store. Made payments of $278 a month to the Roses. T3 Describes mistakes they made when they first started in the business. Built a warehouse and fixed up the upstairs to live in. T4 People would to wake them up in the middle of the night to get groceries for the Sheridan Hotel. Bought one lot for $1000 and added a larger meat market. Had to enlarge shelves. T5 The mine was working and they cashed all the miners paychecks. Harold would drive to Ouray and pick up $10-12,000 in order to have cash for the paychecks. There was no bank in town at that time. T6 Story of someone trying to break in to the store. Describes the incident. Same person also broke into the City Hall that night M. McDonald. J. Irving. T7 Continues talking about a shady character. They only had one attempted robbery in all those years. T8 Stories of incidents that happened through the years. J. Vigil. T9 They were so busy they lost lots of merchandise, as they could not watch everyone. Tell many stories about their customers. T10 Several Navajo Indians lived in town and worked at the mine during 1951 & 1952. A. Watt. T11 Customers always charged their groceries. T12 They talk about the many charge accounts they had. Customers did not pay their accounts in full. Lost lots of money on charge accounts. Everett Morrow. Bill Brown. T13 Ran charge accounts because people did not have the money to buy groceries. Had $50,000 on books when they sold, which they lost. T14 Names various people they employed over the years. F. Conrad. I. Lopez. G. Irving. A. Hughes. F. Goldsworthy. Koskinen boys. T15 Mary McDonald did the books. Grace did the everyday bookkeeping. Describes process of bookkeeping and the charge accounts. People paid every 2 weeks on paydays. T16 Grace remembers working through 1 whole weekend, with out sleep, doing inventory. Speaks of stores today. T17 Talk about produce suppliers and the meat department. T18 Continues stories about being cheated by various people. J. White. T19 They talk about all the changes in town during the years. Did not have money to keep up Social Security payments, so do not receive full benefits now. T20 Harold tells story of soap salesman, and the time they received 2 semi-truck loads of merchandise on the same day. Bud Carrier. T21 Names other grocery stores in town and why they closed. Goldsworthy. Pilcher. T22 New store from Rico tried to run them out of business. T23 Tells why the other stores closed. T24 Harold was operated on. He had cancer of throat after they retired. T25 Describes the Christmas parties they held for the children of the community every year. Gave candy and gifts to over 200 children. Always gave popcorn balls to trick-or-treaters on Halloween. T26 Mentions the many things they did for the community during the years. T27 Grace talks about working hard all her life. People do not work as hard now as they use to. Pakka. T28 Continues talking about how it is now. T29 Speak of depression and the bank closing. Pennasa. T30 Talk about all the various jobs they held during the depression. Hardy. T31 Conclusion End CD2 End

Description: Davine Pera interviews Bob & Oda Alexander on April 17, 1990. At first, the family history is discussed (please see Bob Alexander's genealogy). Here is the transcript of this recording: History and birthdates of Alexanders and their parents. Oda started school in Crawford, Co., then moved to San Miguel in 1906 where she attended school, finishing the 8th grade. She attended high school in Telluride where she graduated in 1913. Bob left Virginia as a young man. He rode the train to Telluride, stayed 1 week, then moved to Montrose for 3 years, then back to Telluride. He worked on a ranch on Turkey Creek mesa for Mr. Lawson for a while, then moved to San Miguel and worked for Mrs. Collins on her dairy ranch. Mrs. Alexander's father died in 1916 but her mother kept the dairy ranch and ran it. Oda related the story of delivering milk as a young girl, her horses ran away and spilled all the milk. She describes how milk was sold to stores and cafes. Talks about how father built the dairy farm at San Miguel. Bob bought John Daniels ranch after he returned from the service. They moved to Deep Creek and ran the dairy there tor 2 years and then moved to Keystone and lived there until 1927. Talks about all the big dairy farms in the area. They speak of delivering milk to the Tomboy mine on tram buckets. Oda describes her trip by train to Telluride from Virginia in 1902 at the age of 8. Recalls crossing the Mississippi River. Her uncle met them at the train in Delta with a team of horses and wagon. It took 2 days to get to Crawford. She lived at Crawford for 1 year, then moved to San Miguel where her father worked for Mc Knights dairy. Oda speaks about her school years and walking from San Miguel to town every day. Her graduating class consisted of 9 girls and 1 boy. Speaks of the courses that were taught including German and Latin. She mentions different classmates. Sometimes her friend's father took them to school on sleigh pulled by horses if weather was bad. The school stopped teaching German when World War l began. Sports consisted of basketball. The teams rode on the train to Ridgeway and Norwood to play their out of town games. Speaks of the train schedule. She rode the train to Montrose to meet Bob when he returned from the service. Bob and Oda were married at San Miguel at her mother's house. Mr. and Mrs. Frank Brown were witnesses. They rode the train to Salt Lake City for their wedding trip. Bob speaks of milking 25 to 30 cows every day by hand, Bob milked 19 cows in 1 hour. They shipped cream to creamery in Montrose on the train in 5 gal. cans and were paid $5 or $6 for one 5 gallon can of cream, and $1 for 10 quarts of milk. Describes the dairy process and how the cooler worked and the procedure for bottling milk in different size bottles. Had to wash bottles every night to be ready for the next day. Memories of running the dairy. They speak of long hours and hard work. In 1927 they went into the sheep business and moved to Norwood in 1939. They speak of different ranches they used for grazing. The Campbell Bros. sheared sheep for them. They received a permit to run sheep on public domain. They went into sheep business after pasteurization of milk became necessary. Speaks of the changes in Telluride now. Bob and Oda talk about traveling to different places after their retirement. They spent their winters in New Mexico for several years. When their daughter graduated from high school, they took the Senior class to Mexico City. They went to Europe in 1956 when their son was stationed in Paris. Bob speaks of the time he spent in the service in World War I. He was ready to go to the front line when the Armistice was signed. Names & Places mentioned: Jacob Alexander, Collins, W. Sampson, Jake Lawson, Frank Brown, John Daniels, Ed Spor, Dr. Brown, Goldsworthy, Oscar Mine, Hoag, Breckenridge, Kelly McKnight, Sam Harman, Emmet Hookman, John McKnight, Claude Jacob, Florence Miser, Mrs. Atterholt, Frank Brown, Campbell Bros., Yates Nardine, J. Porterfield, Finnegan, and Whiteley.
